posts.title: Welcome to my writing
posts.excerpt: A small corner of the internet where I'll think out loud about product, design, and the weird little ideas I can't stop chewing on.
posts.html:
I've been meaning to write publicly for years. Not the LinkedIn kind — the slow, honest kind. The kind you come back to a year later and realise how much you've changed.

This is that corner.

What you'll find here

Notes on building products at Visit Health, lessons from shipping side projects like Fitin Club and Reccos, the occasional rant about design systems, and a lot of thinking in public about what it means to be a non-technical founder in 2026.

How this is built

Every post you read here started as a markdown file in my Obsidian vault. I write. I hit save. A tiny script notices the new file and my website rebuilds itself overnight.

No CMS. No editor UI. Just text → git → live.

That's the kind of plumbing that makes writing feel lightweight again.

More soon.

posts.title: The non-technical founder's unfair advantage
posts.excerpt: For the first time in history, taste compounds faster than code. Here's why that changes who gets to build.
posts.html:
For ten years, "non-technical founder" was a polite way of saying dependent.

You had ideas. Someone else had the keys to the kingdom. You waited, negotiated equity, wrote specs that got half-understood, and shipped things that looked nothing like what lived in your head.

That era is over.

Taste is the new moat

When anyone can generate a React component in thirty seconds, the bottleneck stops being "who can build it" and becomes "who knows what's worth building." That's a taste problem. A systems-thinking problem. A customer-empathy problem.

Those are exactly the muscles a good PM has been training for years without realising they were training for this moment.

What I actually do now

I think in flows. I sketch in Figma. I write specs in plain English. Claude turns them into code. I read the code, push back where it feels wrong, and ship.

The work isn't coding. The work is having a clear opinion about what the thing should feel like — and being able to hold that opinion steady through every step of implementation.

The catch

You still have to understand enough to smell when something is off. A URL in a query string. A database without indexes. A component re-rendering on every keystroke. You don't need to write the fix — you need to notice it exists.

That's not technical debt. That's technical literacy. And it's cheap to acquire if you're paying attention.

What this means

If you've been sitting on an idea waiting for a co-founder, stop. The co-founder is already in your laptop. Your job is to bring the taste.

posts.title: Shipping v2 of this site
posts.excerpt: Why I rebuilt my portfolio as a draggable, collaborative canvas — and what I learned about playful interfaces in the process.
posts.html:
The first version of this site was fine. Tasteful. Safe. It looked like every other PM portfolio in 2025.

That was the problem.

The brief I gave myself

A portfolio is supposed to be proof. Not a résumé in slightly nicer typography — actual evidence of how someone thinks. So I rewrote the brief: every pixel on the homepage should demonstrate something about how I build.

That led to the collaboration toggle. Flip it on and the page stops behaving like a document. The hero letters become draggable. The cards tilt. A second cursor appears — yours — and you can push things around the canvas. It's a live Figma file disguised as a portfolio.

Why draggable

Because I believe the best products feel like they want to be touched. Static sites are a compromise we made when browsers were slow. They aren't anymore.

What broke

The first version used individual letter drag — each "E", "S", "H", "A", "N" was its own draggable. It looked cute in my head and felt chaotic in practice. Word-level drag is the right abstraction.

The sticker on the hero also collapsed into document flow the first time I shipped the hover borders. One-line CSS fix, but it took me embarrassingly long to find.

What I'd do differently

Ship smaller. I held v2 for two weeks trying to get every interaction perfect. Should have pushed the canvas-drag-only version on day three and iterated in public.

What's next

v2.2 is live right now — you're reading it. I added writings (this page), backed by Obsidian files in the repo. Every post you see here started as a note I wrote in bed.
